stimulus: Columnist: Studies consistently show that people who regularly practice meditation report lower stress levels than those who do not. However, these studies rely entirely on self-reporting, and meditators, having invested considerable time in the practice, are motivated to believe that it works. Therefore, meditation almost certainly has no genuine effect on stress.
prompt: The reasoning in the columnist's argument is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that the argument:

choices.A: takes the absence of reliable evidence for a claim to establish that the claim is false
choices.B: relies on studies whose participants are unlikely to be representative of the general population
choices.C: presumes, without justification, that self-reported data can never accurately reflect a person's actual condition
choices.D: criticizes the personal motivations of meditators rather than addressing the substance of their reports
choices.E: mistakes a mere correlation between meditation and reduced stress for evidence of a causal relationship

explanation: The columnist shows only that the evidence is unreliable (self-reported plus motivated believers), which at most licenses 'we don't know whether meditation works.' Concluding it 'almost certainly has no genuine effect' treats absent proof as proof of absence. That two-step leap -- from 'your proof is bad' to 'your claim is false' -- is the flaw.

traps.B: Representativeness is never the columnist's concern; the attack is on the honesty/reliability of self-reports, not on whether the sample generalizes.
traps.C: Overshoots. The argument treats these particular reports as suspect, not all self-reports as inherently worthless. Watch for answers that inflate a narrow claim into a universal one.
traps.D: Tempting, because there is a whiff of attacking motives. But the columnist does engage the substance (the evidence's reliability), and even granting the motive point, the fatal error is the leap to 'false,' not the mention of motive.
traps.E: Describes a real flaw that exists in the wild but not here. The columnist isn't inferring causation from correlation; he's rejecting the studies outright. Classic 170-level trap: a genuine flaw pattern that this argument doesn't commit.

takeaway: Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence. When an argument discredits the proof and then declares the claim false, the correct verdict was only 'unproven.'

stimulus: Economist: The city council claims that the new transit tax will reduce traffic congestion. But most drivers surveyed said they would not switch to public transit even if service improved. So the tax revenue, which is earmarked exclusively for transit improvements, will fail to reduce congestion.
prompt: Which one of the following is an assumption required by the economist's argument?

choices.A: The surveyed drivers accurately predicted their own future behavior.
choices.B: Traffic congestion cannot be reduced unless a substantial number of current drivers switch to public transit.
choices.C: The transit improvements funded by the tax will not attract enough new transit riders to affect congestion.
choices.D: The city council has no other plans to reduce traffic congestion besides the transit tax.
choices.E: Improving transit service is the least effective way to reduce traffic congestion.

explanation: Negate (C): 'the improvements WILL attract enough new riders to affect congestion.' The conclusion collapses instantly, so (C) is required. The gap it plugs: the survey only covers current drivers, but congestion could still fall via new residents, young people who never start driving, or the minority who do switch. The economist quietly assumes none of that adds up.

traps.A: 'Accurately' is too strong for a necessary assumption. Negate it -- 'drivers did not predict perfectly' -- and even small inaccuracy makes the negation true without sinking the argument, which needs the predictions only roughly right. Strong words (accurately, never, only) usually fail the negation test.
traps.B: Wrong mechanism and too strong. Negating it -- 'congestion can be reduced without current drivers switching' -- only opens a possibility; it doesn't assert that possibility operates here, so the argument survives. Not required.
traps.D: Irrelevant to whether THIS tax works. Whatever else the council plans, it doesn't change the tax's own effect on congestion. Negation leaves the argument intact.
traps.E: A comparative claim the argument never needs. The economist only needs this tax to fail, not for it to be the worst of all options. Negating it changes nothing.

takeaway: The negation test: negate a choice; if the argument is destroyed (not merely weakened), it was necessary. Negate logically, not dramatically -- 'not accurately' means 'slightly off,' not 'wildly wrong.' Use it only to break ties between your final two.

stimulus: Archaeologists excavating a 3,000-year-old settlement found that it was crossed by wide, evenly paved roads laid out on a consistent grid. They concluded that the society that built the settlement must have had a centralized authority capable of coordinating large public-works projects.
prompt: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the archaeologists' conclusion?

choices.A: The dwellings within the settlement varied considerably in size and apparent wealth.
choices.B: Several nearby settlements from the same era had no paved roads at all.
choices.C: Building evenly paved roads on a consistent grid requires standardized materials and coordinated labor that independent households acting alone could not have organized.
choices.D: The roads were substantially widened by a later civilization that occupied the site.
choices.E: No written records describing a governing body have survived from the settlement.

explanation: The conclusion needs the paved grid to be a genuine marker of central coordination. (C) forges exactly that link: if grid roads demand organization beyond what scattered households could manage, then their existence really does point to a coordinating authority. It closes the gap between the physical evidence and the inference about social structure.

traps.A: Wealth inequality is a different social fact. Unequal households can exist with or without centralized public-works capacity, so this neither supports nor requires the conclusion.
traps.B: A comparison that shows this settlement was unusual, but not that paved grids require central authority. Being rare isn't the same as requiring coordination.
traps.D: About a later civilization -- irrelevant to what the ORIGINAL builders could organize. Watch for answers that shift the time frame.
traps.E: If anything this weakens the case by removing corroborating evidence of a government.

takeaway: Strengtheners usually bolt the evidence to the conclusion by ruling out the 'could this happen without X?' alternative. Ask what has to be true for the evidence to actually point where the author says.

stimulus: After a new law required restaurants to post the calorie count of each menu item, the average number of calories in the meals customers ordered dropped by five percent over the following year. Clearly, posting calorie counts causes diners to choose lower-calorie meals.
prompt: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?

choices.A: During the same year, a widely publicized national health campaign urged people to reduce the calories they consumed.
choices.B: At some restaurants the calorie counts were printed in small type that was easy to overlook.
choices.C: The posted calorie counts were frequently inaccurate by a small margin.
choices.D: In surveys, most diners said they paid close attention to the posted calorie counts.
choices.E: The law applied only to restaurant chains with twenty or more locations.

explanation: The argument infers causation (posting -> lower-calorie choices) from a before/after drop. (A) supplies a rival cause operating over the very same period: a national campaign could account for the five-percent drop with no help from posted counts. An alternative explanation for the correlation is the sharpest way to weaken a causal claim.

traps.B: If the counts were easy to overlook, the drop happened anyway -- which doesn't clearly cut against the posting-causes-drop claim and could even suggest something else was driving it, but only vaguely. (A) names a concrete competing cause; this doesn't.
traps.C: Small inaccuracies don't undermine the idea that seeing (roughly right) counts changed behavior. Accuracy isn't the causal question.
traps.D: Strengthens -- diners attending to the counts supports the posting-causes-choice story.
traps.E: Scope of the law. Says nothing about whether the counts, rather than something else, caused the drop.

takeaway: To weaken a 'X happened then Y happened, so X caused Y' argument, the highest-value move is a rival cause acting in the same window. Don't get pulled by answers that quibble with the data's precision.

stimulus: If a company's data is encrypted, then hackers cannot read it. FinCorp's data is encrypted. Therefore, FinCorp's customers' personal information is safe from theft.
prompt: The conclusion follows logically if which one of the following is assumed?

choices.A: FinCorp's encrypted data includes its customers' personal information.
choices.B: If hackers cannot read a company's data, then that company's customers' personal information is safe from theft.
choices.C: Only data that is encrypted is safe from theft.
choices.D: Hackers cannot read FinCorp's data.
choices.E: No company whose data is encrypted has ever had customer information stolen.

explanation: Map the terms: premises get us to 'hackers cannot read FinCorp's data,' but the conclusion is about 'customers' information safe from theft.' (B) is the missing bridge -- it converts 'cannot read' directly into 'customers' info safe from theft,' so the conclusion follows with certainty. Sufficient-assumption answers connect the last premise's term to the conclusion's term.

traps.A: Links 'data' to 'customer information,' which feels needed, but even granting it you still never reach 'safe from theft.' A partial bridge is not sufficient.
traps.C: States a necessary condition (safe -> encrypted), the reverse of what's needed. It doesn't let you conclude anything IS safe.
traps.D: Already derivable from the premises (encrypted -> cannot read). Restating a step you already have adds nothing toward 'safe from theft.'
traps.E: Empirical and about companies generally, not the clean conditional bridge; 'robbed in the past' doesn't guarantee FinCorp's customer info is safe now, and it mismatches the conclusion's terms.

takeaway: For Sufficient Assumption, find the new term in the conclusion, find where the premises stop, and pick the choice that welds those two together. It must GUARANTEE the conclusion, not merely make it likely.

stimulus: Every member of the debate club is on the honor roll. No one on the honor roll has failed a class this year. Some members of the debate club are seniors.
prompt: If the statements above are true, which one of the following must also be true?

choices.A: Some seniors have not failed a class this year.
choices.B: No senior has failed a class this year.
choices.C: Everyone on the honor roll is a member of the debate club.
choices.D: All seniors are on the honor roll.
choices.E: Some students on the honor roll are not members of the debate club.

explanation: Chain it: some debate members are seniors; all debate members are on the honor roll; no one on the honor roll has failed. So those senior debate members are on the honor roll and therefore have not failed -- giving us at least some seniors who have not failed a class. That's exactly (A).

traps.B: Too strong. We only know about seniors who are in the debate club; non-debate seniors are entirely uncovered, so we can't say NO senior failed.
traps.C: Reverses the conditional. 'All debate members are on the honor roll' does not mean all honor-roll students are debate members.
traps.D: Unsupported -- nothing places all seniors on the honor roll, only the debate-club ones.
traps.E: Possible but not necessary. The honor roll could consist entirely of debate members for all we're told, so this doesn't have to be true.

takeaway: Must-Be-True answers can't outrun the premises. Distinguish 'some' from 'all,' and never reverse a conditional. If a choice could be false in even one consistent scenario, it's out.

stimulus: Everyone who exercises regularly is healthy. Maria is healthy. Therefore, Maria must exercise regularly.
prompt: Which one of the following arguments contains flawed reasoning most similar to that in the argument above?

choices.A: Every cake sold at this bakery contains sugar. This cake contains sugar. So this cake must have been sold at the bakery.
choices.B: Every senator voted for the bill. Lee is a senator. So Lee voted for the bill.
choices.C: All dogs are mammals, and no mammals are birds. So no dog is a bird.
choices.D: If it rains, the game is canceled. It did not rain. So the game was not canceled.
choices.E: Most successful entrepreneurs wake up early. Tom wakes up early. So Tom is probably a successful entrepreneur.

explanation: The stimulus affirms the consequent: from 'exercise -> healthy' and 'healthy,' it wrongly concludes 'exercises.' (A) has the identical structure: from 'bakery -> sugar' and 'sugar,' it wrongly concludes 'bakery.' Both take a sufficient condition being met on the wrong end and run the conditional backward.

traps.B: Valid modus ponens (senator -> voted; is a senator; so voted). Not flawed at all.
traps.C: Valid categorical reasoning. No error to parallel.
traps.D: Denies the antecedent (a different flaw). Structurally not a match for affirming the consequent.
traps.E: A statistical/converse slip, but it uses 'most' and 'probably,' not the universal-plus-certain form of the stimulus. Parallel questions demand a match in logical FORM, and the certainty level differs.

takeaway: For Parallel Flaw, name the exact error first (here: affirming the consequent), then find the choice with the same skeleton. Match the logical form and the certainty level -- 'most/probably' can't parallel 'all/must.'

stimulus: Principle: One should not condemn a decision as imprudent unless one can identify a specific alternative course of action that would have produced a better outcome.
prompt: Which one of the following judgments conforms most closely to the principle stated above?

choices.A: Dana declined to call the mayor's budget imprudent, because she could not point to any specific alternative that would have produced a better result.
choices.B: Raj condemned the company's investment as imprudent even though he admitted he had no better strategy in mind.
choices.C: Sam praised the committee's plan as prudent mainly because it was popular with the public.
choices.D: Lee criticized the board's decision on the ground that it was unpopular with employees.
choices.E: Kim, who had identified a clearly superior alternative, chose not to criticize the decision at all.

explanation: The principle forbids condemning a decision as imprudent WITHOUT a specific better alternative in hand. Dana lacks such an alternative and therefore refrains from condemning -- precisely the behavior the principle prescribes. It's the cleanest application of the rule.

traps.B: A direct violation: Raj condemns as imprudent with no better strategy. Conforming means following the principle, not breaking it.
traps.C: About praising something as prudent for being popular -- the principle governs condemnation, not praise, and says nothing about popularity.
traps.D: 'Unpopular' is not 'imprudent.' The principle addresses imprudence judgments, so criticizing on popularity grounds is outside its scope.
traps.E: Not a violation, but not an application either. The principle only bars condemning WITHOUT an alternative; it never requires you to condemn when you have one, so Kim's silence neither follows from nor tests the rule.

takeaway: Conform questions reward the choice that the principle actually governs and endorses. Check the principle's trigger (condemning as imprudent) and its scope -- praise, popularity, or optional actions fall outside it.

stimulus: Critics charge that the district's new curriculum lowers academic standards. But when the curriculum was piloted in several schools last year, students' standardized test scores rose significantly. The critics are therefore mistaken.
prompt: The argument proceeds by:

choices.A: questioning the motives of those who advance the opposing position
choices.B: appealing to the judgment of experts on academic standards
choices.C: presenting evidence of an outcome that would be unlikely if the opposing position were correct
choices.D: pointing out that the opposing position is supported by no evidence whatsoever
choices.E: showing that the opposing position rests on an ambiguous use of the term 'standards'

explanation: The argument rebuts the critics by citing rising test scores -- an outcome you'd expect NOT to see if the curriculum really lowered standards. Producing a result that clashes with a prediction implied by the opposing view is the method here.

traps.A: No motives are impugned; the response is about results, not the critics' character.
traps.B: No expert authority is invoked; the evidence is test-score data, not someone's judgment.
traps.D: The argument doesn't claim the critics have no evidence; it offers positive counter-evidence of its own.
traps.E: No ambiguity in 'standards' is exposed. The rebuttal is empirical, not semantic.

takeaway: Method questions ask HOW, not WHETHER, the argument works. Describe the move in the abstract -- here, 'counters a claim with evidence contrary to what the claim predicts' -- before matching it to a choice.

stimulus:
Mara: Public libraries should shift their priorities toward digital resources. Physical books are increasingly obsolete, and limited budgets are better spent on e-books and databases.
Eli: Many patrons still depend on physical books, and libraries serve their communities best by maintaining strong print collections.
prompt: Mara and Eli disagree over whether:

choices.A: public libraries currently offer any digital resources
choices.B: some library patrons make use of physical books
choices.C: public libraries should receive larger budgets than they now have
choices.D: libraries should shift their priorities toward digital resources at the expense of print collections
choices.E: digital resources cost less to provide than print collections do

explanation: Mara explicitly advocates shifting priorities toward digital; Eli argues libraries serve best by keeping strong print collections -- a direct clash over whether to make that shift. (D) is the one claim Mara would affirm and Eli would deny, which is the test for the point at issue.

traps.A: Neither speaker addresses whether libraries currently offer digital resources; there's no disagreement to locate here.
traps.B: Eli asserts patrons use physical books and Mara never denies it. Agreement (or non-engagement) isn't disagreement.
traps.C: Budget size isn't discussed by either; both mention spending only in passing, without opposing views on total budget.
traps.E: Cost comparison is never raised. Mara mentions budgets but neither states that digital is cheaper, so they don't disagree about it.

takeaway: The point at issue is the single statement one speaker affirms and the other denies. Test each choice: would BOTH take a stand, on OPPOSITE sides? If either is silent or agrees, eliminate.

stimulus: A city installed automated cameras at its busiest intersections in order to deter drivers from running red lights. Yet in the year after the cameras were installed, the number of red-light violations officially recorded at those intersections rose sharply, even though surveys showed that drivers were being more cautious than before.
prompt: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy described above?

choices.A: The cameras occasionally malfunctioned and failed to capture some violations.
choices.B: Before the cameras were installed, the great majority of red-light violations went undetected and unrecorded; the cameras captured many violations that previously would never have been counted.
choices.C: Overall traffic volume at the intersections declined during the year after the cameras were installed.
choices.D: Drivers in neighboring cities without cameras also frequently ran red lights.
choices.E: The fines imposed for running a red light were increased at the same time the cameras were installed.

explanation: The puzzle is that RECORDED violations rose while actual driving got more cautious. (B) dissolves it by separating the two: previously most violations were simply never caught, so the recorded count was artificially low. Cameras count what used to slip through, so recorded violations can jump even as real red-light running falls.

traps.A: Malfunctions would UNDER-count violations, pushing the recorded number down -- the opposite of what needs explaining.
traps.C: Fewer cars should mean fewer violations, deepening the mystery of why recorded violations rose.
traps.D: Other cities are irrelevant to why THIS city's recorded count and driver behavior moved in opposite directions.
traps.E: Higher fines add deterrence, which should lower violations -- it sharpens the paradox rather than resolving it.

takeaway: Resolve questions often hinge on a hidden distinction. Here it's 'recorded' vs. 'actual.' The right answer reconciles both facts at once; wrong answers usually push one fact the wrong way and worsen the puzzle.

stimulus: Some people argue that zoos are unethical simply because they confine animals. It is true that zoos keep animals in enclosures. But accredited zoos fund conservation programs that have rescued several species from extinction, and they educate millions of visitors about wildlife each year. So the benefits of accredited zoos outweigh the ethical costs of confining animals.
prompt: Which one of the following most accurately expresses the main conclusion of the argument?

choices.A: Zoos keep animals in enclosures.
choices.B: Accredited zoos fund conservation programs that have rescued species from extinction.
choices.C: Zoos are unethical because they confine animals.
choices.D: Accredited zoos educate millions of visitors about wildlife each year.
choices.E: The benefits of accredited zoos outweigh the ethical costs of confining animals.

explanation: Everything else in the passage supports the final sentence. The conservation and education points are reasons; 'zoos keep animals in enclosures' is a concession. The 'So' signals the author's own claim: the benefits outweigh the ethical costs. That's the main conclusion.

traps.A: A conceded premise ('It is true that...'), granted only to be outweighed. Concessions are never the main point.
traps.B: A supporting premise -- one of the benefits offered as evidence for the conclusion.
traps.C: The opposing view the author argues AGAINST, not the author's own conclusion.
traps.D: Another supporting premise, not the claim everything else is marshaled to establish.

takeaway: Find the main conclusion by asking what everything else supports. Use the 'therefore' test, watch for concessions ('it is true that...'), and don't mistake the view being rebutted for the author's claim.

stimulus: Advertisement: In a recent test, BrightSmile toothpaste was compared with the leading brand. Among the dentists who expressed a preference, more chose BrightSmile than chose the leading brand. So BrightSmile is the toothpaste that most dentists prefer.
prompt: The reasoning in the advertisement is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it:

choices.A: draws a conclusion about dentists generally from a premise about only those dentists who expressed a preference
choices.B: takes for granted that dentists' preferences are a reliable guide to a toothpaste's quality
choices.C: presumes that BrightSmile is superior simply because it is newer than the leading brand
choices.D: overlooks the possibility that the leading brand is preferred by many consumers who are not dentists
choices.E: relies on the opinions of people whose expertise is unrelated to the claim being made

explanation: The premise is only about dentists 'who expressed a preference' -- a group that may be a small, unrepresentative slice of all dentists. The conclusion leaps to 'most dentists.' Those with no preference are silently dropped, so the sample can't support a claim about dentists as a whole.

traps.B: A different assumption. The conclusion is about what dentists prefer, not about quality, so whether preference tracks quality is beside the point.
traps.C: Novelty is never offered as the basis for the conclusion; the ad rests on the preference test, not on BrightSmile being newer.
traps.D: The argument's conclusion is specifically about dentists, and it does compare the two brands. Non-dentist consumers aren't the gap; the unrepresentative dentist sample is.
traps.E: Dentists are relevant experts on toothpaste, so this misdescribes the problem. The flaw is the sampling, not the choice of experts.

takeaway: Watch the quantifier shift: 'most who expressed a preference' is not 'most' of the whole group. Sampling only the opinionated silently distorts the conclusion.

stimulus: Researchers found that people who take short daytime naps score higher on memory tests than people who do not nap. They concluded that napping improves memory.
prompt: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the researchers' conclusion?

choices.A: Nappers and non-nappers in the study reported similar levels of daily stress.
choices.B: When participants were randomly assigned either to nap or to stay awake, those assigned to nap subsequently scored higher on memory tests.
choices.C: The memory tests given to nappers and non-nappers were identical in length and format.
choices.D: A few of the non-nappers scored higher on the memory tests than some of the nappers.
choices.E: Napping is most common among retirees, who tend to have more unstructured free time.

explanation: The conclusion is causal (napping improves memory), but the evidence is a mere correlation. Random assignment is the strongest possible support: because who naps is decided by chance, differences in later memory scores can't be explained by pre-existing differences or reverse causation. That directly establishes the causal link.

traps.A: Helpful, since it rules out one confound (stress), but far weaker than randomization. It controls a single alternative, not the causal question as a whole.
traps.C: A methodological detail about test fairness. It doesn't address whether napping, rather than something else, caused the higher scores.
traps.D: If anything this cuts slightly against the conclusion by showing the effect isn't uniform. It doesn't strengthen the causal claim.
traps.E: Introduces a confound: retirees differ in many ways (age, routine) that could affect memory. This points to an alternative explanation, weakening rather than strengthening.

takeaway: To turn a correlation into a cause, the gold standard is randomization -- it rules out reverse causation and confounds in one move. Controlling a single variable helps, but less.

stimulus: City official: Since we installed brighter streetlights downtown last year, reported nighttime crime there has fallen by 20 percent. The brighter lighting is clearly responsible for the decline, so we should install it citywide.
prompt: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the official's argument?

choices.A: During the same year, the police department assigned a substantial number of additional officers to patrol the downtown area at night.
choices.B: The brighter streetlights consume noticeably more electricity than the lights they replaced.
choices.C: Several downtown residents complained that the brighter lighting made it harder for them to sleep.
choices.D: Brighter streetlights of the type installed downtown need to be replaced more frequently than standard streetlights.
choices.E: In the years just before the brighter lights were installed, downtown nighttime crime had been rising steadily.

explanation: The argument infers that lighting caused the drop from a before/after decline. (A) supplies a rival cause operating in the very same period: more officers on patrol could account for the 20 percent decline with no help from the lighting. An alternative cause in the same window is the sharpest weakener of a causal claim.

traps.B: The electricity cost of the lights says nothing about whether they caused the crime drop. It's a downside of the intervention, not a challenge to the causal claim.
traps.C: Sleep complaints are a side effect, irrelevant to whether the lighting reduced crime.
traps.D: Maintenance frequency is about cost, not causation. It doesn't bear on why crime fell.
traps.E: A prior rising trend that then reversed after the lights went in would, if anything, support the lighting's effect -- the opposite of weakening.

takeaway: The classic causal weakener is an alternative cause acting in the same period. Costs and side effects of the intervention are irrelevant to whether it produced the effect.

stimulus: If a policy is both fair and effective, then it should be adopted. The proposed carbon tax is effective. Therefore, the proposed carbon tax should be adopted.
prompt: The conclusion follows logically if which one of the following is assumed?

choices.A: The proposed carbon tax is fair.
choices.B: Only policies that should be adopted are both fair and effective.
choices.C: Most policies that are effective should be adopted.
choices.D: If a policy is fair, then it should be adopted.
choices.E: The proposed carbon tax is more effective than any alternative policy.

explanation: The rule fires only for policies that are both fair AND effective. We're told the tax is effective; the missing piece is 'fair.' Assume (A) and the tax is fair and effective, so by the rule it should be adopted -- the conclusion follows with certainty.

traps.B: Runs the conditional backward (adopt -> fair and effective). That direction lets you conclude nothing about what SHOULD be adopted.
traps.C: 'Most' can't guarantee anything about this particular tax, which could be one of the exceptions. Too weak to make the conclusion follow.
traps.D: A rule that never fires here: we aren't told the tax is fair, so 'fair -> adopt' can't be triggered. It doesn't deliver the conclusion.
traps.E: Being the most effective option doesn't establish fairness, which is the term the rule requires. The conclusion still wouldn't follow.

takeaway: Sufficient assumption: supply the exact term the rule needs to fire -- here the tax must be 'fair.' Rules that run backward, or that never trigger, don't get you there.

stimulus: Every painting in the Ruiz collection is either a landscape or a portrait, but not both. More than half of the paintings in the collection are landscapes. Some of the portraits in the collection are valuable.
prompt: If the statements above are true, which one of the following must also be true?

choices.A: Some valuable paintings in the collection are portraits.
choices.B: Most of the valuable paintings in the collection are landscapes.
choices.C: No landscape in the collection is valuable.
choices.D: Most of the paintings in the collection are portraits.
choices.E: All of the valuable paintings in the collection are portraits.

explanation: We're told some portraits are valuable. Those paintings are both portraits and valuable, so 'some valuable paintings are portraits' must be true -- it's just the same 'some' statement read the other way around.

traps.B: We know most PAINTINGS are landscapes, but nothing about most VALUABLE paintings. You can't transfer 'most' from one group to a different one.
traps.C: Unsupported. That some portraits are valuable tells us nothing about whether any landscapes are; landscapes could be valuable too.
traps.D: Contradicts the premise that more than half the paintings are landscapes.
traps.E: Too strong. 'Some portraits are valuable' does not mean every valuable painting is a portrait; valuable landscapes aren't ruled out.

takeaway: 'Some X are Y' yields only 'some Y are X' -- never 'most' or 'all.' And don't smuggle 'most paintings' into a claim about 'most valuable paintings.'

stimulus: Principle: A person deserves credit for an achievement only if the achievement resulted from that person's own effort rather than from luck.
prompt: Which one of the following judgments conforms most closely to the principle stated above?

choices.A: Nadia became wealthy purely by winning a lottery, so she deserves no credit for her wealth.
choices.B: Ravi became wealthy purely by inheriting a fortune, so he deserves credit for his wealth.
choices.C: Because Mia worked hard to write her novel, the novel must be of high quality.
choices.D: Since Leo deserves credit for his promotion, his promotion must have resulted from his own effort.
choices.E: Ana succeeded through years of diligent practice, so she deserves no credit for her success.

explanation: Nadia's wealth came from luck (a lottery), not effort. The principle says credit is deserved only when an achievement results from effort, so it withholds credit here -- exactly the judgment (A) reaches.

traps.B: A direct violation: inheritance is luck, so the principle would withhold credit, yet (B) grants it.
traps.C: Confuses credit with quality. The principle governs whether someone deserves credit, not whether the work is good.
traps.D: Runs the principle backward. 'Credit only if effort' does not license inferring effort from the fact that credit is deserved.
traps.E: Contradicts the principle: effort-based success is precisely what should earn credit, yet (E) denies it.

takeaway: Apply a principle in its stated direction. 'Credit only if effort' withholds credit for luck-based achievement; it does NOT let you infer effort from credit (that reverses it).

stimulus: If the bridge is safe, then it passed inspection. But the bridge did not pass inspection. Therefore, the bridge is not safe.
prompt: The pattern of reasoning above is most similar to that in which one of the following?

choices.A: If this recipe is correct, then it lists baking soda. This recipe does not list baking soda. So this recipe is not correct.
choices.B: If the alarm is working, then it will sound during a fire. The alarm sounded. So the alarm is working.
choices.C: If an animal is a dog, then it is a mammal. This animal is a mammal. So this animal is a dog.
choices.D: If the plant is healthy, then it has green leaves. The plant is healthy. So it has green leaves.
choices.E: If the store is open, then its lights are on. The store is not open. So its lights are not on.

explanation: The stimulus is valid modus tollens: given 'safe -> passed' and 'not passed,' it correctly concludes 'not safe.' (A) has the identical form: 'correct -> lists baking soda' and 'does not list baking soda' yields 'not correct.' Deny the consequent, deny the antecedent.

traps.B: Affirms the consequent (it sounded, so it works) -- an invalid form, not the valid modus tollens of the stimulus.
traps.C: Also affirms the consequent (mammal, so dog). Wrong structure.
traps.D: Valid, but it's modus ponens (affirm the antecedent), a different skeleton from the stimulus's modus tollens.
traps.E: Denies the antecedent (not open, so lights off) -- invalid, and structurally unlike the stimulus.

takeaway: Parallel reasoning demands the same logical form. This is modus tollens; modus ponens, affirming the consequent, and denying the antecedent are all different skeletons.

stimulus: Skeptic: You claim the ancient manuscript is genuine because its ink chemically matches ink from the period. But forgers working in that same period had access to exactly that ink. So the ink evidence does nothing to establish that the manuscript is authentic.
prompt: The skeptic's argument proceeds by:

choices.A: arguing that a piece of evidence fails to support a conclusion because it is equally compatible with a competing explanation
choices.B: presenting new evidence that directly proves the manuscript is a forgery
choices.C: attacking the character of those who believe the manuscript is genuine
choices.D: pointing out that the argument depends on a term that has never been clearly defined
choices.E: conceding that the manuscript is authentic while objecting to the way that conclusion was reached

explanation: The skeptic grants that the ink matches the period, then observes that period forgers had the same ink -- so the evidence fits authenticity and forgery equally well. Because it can't distinguish the two, it supports neither. That's the method: neutralize evidence by showing it's equally consistent with a rival hypothesis.

traps.B: The skeptic never proves forgery; the point is only that the ink evidence is inconclusive, not that the manuscript is fake.
traps.C: No one's character is attacked; the argument is about what the evidence can show.
traps.D: No ambiguous or undefined term is at issue. The objection is about the evidence, not the wording.
traps.E: The skeptic does not concede authenticity; the whole point is that authenticity remains unestablished.

takeaway: A powerful rebuttal: show the evidence fits a rival hypothesis just as well, so it can't favor either -- without having to prove the rival true.

stimulus: A company replaced its annual performance reviews with frequent informal feedback, expecting employee satisfaction with the feedback process to rise. Yet a year later, surveyed employees reported lower satisfaction with the feedback process than before -- even though managers were now giving feedback far more often.
prompt: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy?

choices.A: Under the new system, the frequent feedback was almost entirely critical and rarely acknowledged employees' successes.
choices.B: Preparing for the old annual reviews had been time-consuming for managers.
choices.C: Employees at several other companies also reported low satisfaction with their feedback processes.
choices.D: The new feedback system was considerably cheaper for the company to administer.
choices.E: Employees generally prefer receiving feedback in person rather than in writing.

explanation: The puzzle is that MORE feedback led to LESS satisfaction. (A) resolves it by revealing the hidden variable -- quality, not quantity. A steady stream of purely critical feedback that ignores good work would naturally lower satisfaction, no matter how frequent it is.

traps.B: About the burden on managers under the OLD system -- it doesn't explain why employees became less satisfied with the new one.
traps.C: Other companies' experiences are irrelevant to why THIS company's satisfaction fell.
traps.D: Cost savings to the company say nothing about employees' satisfaction with the feedback.
traps.E: Informal feedback is typically delivered in person, so a preference for in-person feedback would predict HIGHER satisfaction -- deepening the puzzle.

takeaway: Resolve: the answer must explain both facts at once. More feedback can lower satisfaction when the hidden variable -- here, relentlessly negative content -- is doing the work.

stimulus: Although solar panels are expensive to install, they pay for themselves within a decade through energy savings. Homeowners who plan to stay in their homes for the long term should therefore install them. Those planning to move soon, of course, may not recoup the cost.
prompt: The claim that solar panels are expensive to install plays which one of the following roles in the argument?

choices.A: It is a consideration the argument acknowledges but contends is outweighed, for the relevant group of homeowners, by a countervailing benefit.
choices.B: It is the main conclusion that the rest of the argument is designed to support.
choices.C: It is offered as evidence that homeowners should not install solar panels.
choices.D: It is a premise supporting the claim that solar panels pay for themselves within a decade.
choices.E: It is an unstated assumption on which the argument's conclusion depends.

explanation: 'Although solar panels are expensive' is a concession: the argument grants the drawback, then argues that for long-term homeowners the decade-long payback outweighs it. So the claim is an acknowledged consideration that gets overridden, not a premise or conclusion.

traps.B: The main conclusion is that long-term homeowners should install panels, not that panels are expensive. The expense is what the argument works past.
traps.C: The argument recommends installing for long-term owners, so the expense is not offered to oppose installation; it's conceded and outweighed.
traps.D: The expense doesn't support the payback claim -- if anything they're in tension. It isn't a premise for that claim.
traps.E: It is explicitly stated ('Although solar panels are expensive'), so it can't be an unstated assumption.

takeaway: Role questions: place the claim in the argument's structure. A conceded drawback ('although X') is acknowledged, then outweighed -- neither the conclusion nor a hidden assumption.

stimulus:
Ari: Standardized tests should be abolished, because they measure test-taking skill more than they measure real knowledge.
Bina: Standardized tests are valuable precisely because, unlike subjective grades, they let us compare students fairly across different schools.
prompt: Ari and Bina disagree over whether:

choices.A: standardized tests primarily measure test-taking skill
choices.B: standardized tests should continue to be used
choices.C: the grades teachers assign are subjective
choices.D: students at different schools should be compared with one another
choices.E: possessing real knowledge is important

explanation: Ari concludes standardized tests should be abolished; Bina defends them as valuable and worth keeping. That is the one claim Ari would deny and Bina would affirm -- whether the tests should continue to be used.

traps.A: Only Ari makes a claim about what the tests measure. Bina never addresses it, so it isn't a point of disagreement.
traps.C: Bina asserts grades are subjective; Ari says nothing about grades. No clash.
traps.D: Bina implies comparison across schools is worthwhile; Ari doesn't take a position on it, so they don't disagree about it.
traps.E: Neither speaker disputes that real knowledge matters -- Ari even appeals to it. Not at issue.

takeaway: The point at issue is the single claim one speaker affirms and the other denies. If either speaker is silent on it, it isn't the disagreement -- even when it's mentioned.

stimulus: Our new hiring software screens resumes far faster than human recruiters can. Last quarter, positions filled using the software were filled in half the time it used to take. So the software makes our hiring process more efficient.
prompt: The answer to which one of the following questions would be most useful in evaluating the argument?

choices.A: Did the employees hired using the software stay and perform as well as those hired by human recruiters?
choices.B: How many resumes can the software screen in a single hour?
choices.C: Do the human recruiters find the software pleasant to use?
choices.D: Are other companies in the industry using similar hiring software?
choices.E: Were most of the positions filled last quarter entry-level positions?

explanation: The argument treats 'filled faster' as 'more efficient.' But speed is only a genuine efficiency gain if the fast hires are also good hires -- if they underperform or quit, the company loses more than it saved. Knowing whether the software's hires performed and stayed as well as recruiters' hires could confirm or overturn the conclusion.

traps.B: This just restates the software's speed, the very premise already granted. It can't test whether speed amounts to real efficiency.
traps.C: Recruiters' enjoyment of the tool is irrelevant to whether hiring became more efficient.
traps.D: What other companies do has no bearing on whether the software improved efficiency here.
traps.E: The seniority of the positions doesn't tell us whether the faster hiring was actually more efficient.

takeaway: Evaluate questions: find the question whose answer could swing the argument either way. 'Fast' only means 'efficient' if the fast hires are also good hires -- so hire quality is the pivot.

stimulus: Everyone who passed the certification exam was given a raise. Some of the employees who passed the exam were later promoted. Raises were also given to some employees who did not pass the exam.
prompt: If the statements above are true, which one of the following must also be true?

choices.A: Some employees who were given a raise were later promoted.
choices.B: Every employee who was given a raise passed the certification exam.
choices.C: Every employee who passed the certification exam was later promoted.
choices.D: Some employees who did not pass the exam were later promoted.
choices.E: No employee who was promoted had been given a raise.

explanation: Some who passed the exam were promoted; everyone who passed got a raise. So those promoted employees had received raises -- meaning some employees who got a raise were later promoted. That chain is airtight.

traps.B: Reverses the relationship. Passing guarantees a raise, but raises also went to some who didn't pass, so not every raise-getter passed.
traps.C: Only SOME who passed were promoted; the premise never says all of them were.
traps.D: Unsupported. Nothing links the employees who didn't pass to being promoted.
traps.E: Contradicts what we derived: the promoted passers did get raises, so it's false that no promoted employee had a raise.

takeaway: Chain only what's given. 'All passers got raises' does not mean 'all raise-getters passed' -- raises went to others too. And keep 'some' as 'some.'

stimulus: Editorial: The city should build its new stadium downtown rather than in the suburbs. A downtown stadium would draw fans to nearby restaurants and shops before and after games, giving a valuable boost to local businesses.
prompt: Which one of the following is an assumption required by the editorial's argument?

choices.A: Fans attending games at a downtown stadium would spend money at businesses near the stadium.
choices.B: A stadium built in the suburbs would not boost the businesses near it as much as a downtown stadium would boost downtown businesses.
choices.C: Downtown businesses are currently struggling more than suburban businesses are.
choices.D: The stadium will attract large crowds to every single game it hosts.
choices.E: Boosting local businesses is the single most important factor in choosing where to build a stadium.

explanation: The editorial recommends downtown OVER the suburbs on the strength of a benefit to nearby businesses. Negate (B): a suburban stadium would boost its neighboring businesses just as much. Then the business-boost gives no reason to prefer downtown, and the argument collapses. So (B) is required -- the argument assumes the benefit isn't matched in the suburbs.

traps.A: This restates a premise (the boost comes from fan spending); it's given, not an assumption the argument needs supplied.
traps.C: The argument doesn't depend on downtown businesses being needier. Negate it -- they're not struggling more -- and a boost is still a boost; the argument survives.
traps.D: 'Every single game' and 'large crowds' are far stronger than the argument needs; some boost suffices. Negating it doesn't sink the argument.
traps.E: The argument needs the boost to be a relevant reason, not the single most important factor. This overstates what's required.

takeaway: When an argument recommends X over Y because X has a benefit, it assumes Y wouldn't deliver a comparable benefit. Test that comparison -- it's usually the necessary assumption.
